Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic. I work part-time and have a lot going on, so I couldn't stick to a rigid schedule. I'd usually carve out time on weekends or late evenings when things calmed down, listening to lecture recordings while doing other tasks. Sometimes I'd have intense study sessions of a few hours, then go days without touching course materials. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work for me.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Deadlines definitely drove a lot of my engagement—I'd suddenly become very focused when something was due. During quieter weeks at work, I could study more regularly, but when things got hectic, the course took a backseat. I learned to be flexible and forgive myself for the inconsistency rather than stress about it.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing—that anxiety about falling behind would push me to at least look. Occasionally I'd log in with good intentions to get ahead but end up just browsing without really diving deep into anything.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every few days, usually Sunday evenings. It became part of my weekly wind-down routine, listening to lectures while preparing for the week ahead. But that habit broke when work got demanding, and I never quite got back to that consistency. The routine was nice while it lasted though.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded sessions were absolute lifesavers for me since I learn best by listening. I could play them while commuting or doing chores, which made studying possible when I otherwise wouldn't have time. The discussion forums were occasionally useful too, seeing how other students interpreted things helped clarify concepts when I was confused.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and lengthy PDFs, honestly. Without reliable internet at home, downloading and reading dense documents felt like too much effort compared to just listening to lectures. I also skipped a lot of the optional quizzes because they felt tedious and I was already stretched thin. If something wasn't clearly connected to my grade, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to exams or assignments. I also valued anything I could engage with passively—like audio content I could absorb while multitasking. If something required me to sit down with full concentration for extended periods, it had to be absolutely essential for me to prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so they got done no matter what. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got bumped when time was tight. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping optional content that might have deepened my understanding, but survival mode meant focusing only on essentials.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them repeatedly on my phone during commutes or at work during breaks. I also made voice memos summarizing key concepts and played those back to myself. My handwritten notes from listening sessions were probably my most valuable study resource, and none of that showed up in the LMS.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was actively studying—reviewing my notes, re-listening to downloaded lectures, talking through concepts out loud—but my LMS activity would show almost nothing. I'd also study with a classmate occasionally, quizzing each other verbally, which was incredibly helpful but completely invisible online. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch or listen to something once online, download it, then return to it multiple times offline. Some lectures I listened to four or five times while doing other things, really letting the concepts sink in. That repetition was crucial for my learning, but the system only registered that single initial access.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough—work got really demanding and my anxiety spiked. I felt overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind and that made it even harder to engage. The boredom with certain topics also made it difficult to motivate myself. I had to actively use mindfulness techniques to manage the anxiety and push through those difficult weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between sleep and studying, and sometimes sleep won. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about work, feeling anxious about deadlines. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't really learning anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around them was paralyzing sometimes—I'd think about starting but feel so overwhelmed that I'd convince myself I'd do it later. Boring topics were especially easy to avoid. I knew this hurt my assignment scores, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before something was due, then go quiet for days. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of deadlines was the only thing that consistently overcame my inertia and anxiety. Without that external pressure, I struggled to engage proactively.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage with similar content. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that area for a while. I tried to use the mindfulness techniques I learned to process feedback more constructively, which helped somewhat.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. The flexibility meant I could adapt it to my chaotic schedule, but it also meant I could procrastinate easily. Having eleven courses to juggle made it hard to give each one proper attention. I appreciated that lectures were available asynchronously since attending live sessions was nearly impossible for me.
Q19: The availability of audio content made engagement much easier for my learning style. However, the heavy reliance on written materials and readings was challenging since that's not how I learn best. The assignment structure felt disconnected from the exam content sometimes, which was frustrating—I could ace exams but struggled with assignments. More alignment there would have helped.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High activity might mean someone is searching, exploring, trying to understand—but struggling to produce polished work. For me, anxiety made it hard to submit things even when I understood the material. Sometimes you're doing a lot of learning that doesn't translate into submitted assignments due to perfectionism or fear of judgment.
Q21: That's basically my story with exams. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I scored really well on exams because my actual learning happened offline. I'd internalize concepts through repeated listening and verbal processing, none of which the LMS could track. Low visible activity doesn't mean low engagement—it might just mean engagement is happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, submission times. That's useful for identifying students who might be struggling to access materials. But logs miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone is actually absorbing content. They capture presence but not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means the material is actually getting into my head and making sense, regardless of how that happens. It's about genuine curiosity and effort to understand, not just clicking through screens. For me, engagement often looks like listening to lectures repeatedly, talking through concepts, making connections—active mental processing, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS would never show my anxiety levels, how many times I listened to downloaded lectures, or the verbal studying I did with classmates. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practice that helped me manage stress and stay in the course. It also wouldn't show that despite low attendance and assignment completion, I deeply understood the material—my exam score reflects learning the data doesn't capture.
